(**HRM**), is the capital and most populous municipality of the Canadian province of [Nova Scotia](/wiki/Nova_Scotia "Nova Scotia"), and the most populous municipality in [Atlantic Canada](/wiki/Atlantic_Canada "Atlantic Canada"). It consists of four former municipalities that were [amalgamated in 1996](/wiki/Amalgamation_of_the_Halifax_Regional_Municipality "Amalgamation of the Halifax Regional Municipality"): [Halifax](/wiki/History_of_Halifax,_Nova_Scotia "History of Halifax, Nova Scotia"), [Dartmouth](/wiki/Dartmouth,_Nova_Scotia "Dartmouth, Nova Scotia"), [Bedford](/wiki/Bedford,_Nova_Scotia "Bedford, Nova Scotia"), and [Halifax County](/wiki/Halifax_County,_Nova_Scotia "Halifax County, Nova Scotia").

**Halifax**, officially the **Halifax Regional Municipality** (**HRM**), is the capital and most populous municipality of the Canadian province of [Nova Scotia](/wiki/Nova_Scotia "Nova Scotia"), and the most populous municipality in [Atlantic Canada](/wiki/Atlantic_Canada "Atlantic Canada"). It consists of four former municipalities that were [amalgamated in 1996](/wiki/Amalgamation_of_the_Halifax_Regional_Municipality "Amalgamation of the Halifax Regional Municipality"): [Halifax](/wiki/History_of_Halifax,_Nova_Scotia "History of Halifax, Nova Scotia"), [Dartmouth](/wiki/Dartmouth,_Nova_Scotia "Dartmouth, Nova Scotia"), [Bedford](/wiki/Bedford,_Nova_Scotia "Bedford, Nova Scotia"), and [Halifax County](/wiki/Halifax_County,_Nova_Scotia "Halifax County, Nova Scotia").

Halifax is an economic centre of Atlantic Canada, home to a concentration of government offices and private companies. Major employers include the [Department of National Defence](/wiki/Department_of_National_Defence_(Canada) "Department of National Defence (Canada)"), [Dalhousie University](/wiki/Dalhousie_University "Dalhousie University"), [Nova Scotia Health Authority](/wiki/Nova_Scotia_Health_Authority "Nova Scotia Health Authority"), [Saint Mary's University](/wiki/Saint_Mary%27s_University_(Halifax) "Saint Mary's University (Halifax)"), the [Halifax Shipyard](/wiki/Halifax_Shipyard "Halifax Shipyard"), various levels of government, and the [Port of Halifax](/wiki/Port_of_Halifax "Port of Halifax"). Resource industries found in rural areas of the municipality include agriculture, fishing, mining, forestry, and natural gas extraction. As of 2024, it is estimated that the population of the Halifax [CMA](/wiki/Census_Metropolitan_Area "Census Metropolitan Area") was 530,167,[[6]](#cite_note-6) with 348,634 people in its [urban area](/wiki/Urban_area "Urban area").[[3]](#cite_note-statcan2-3)

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Halifax,_Nova_Scotia&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The area was called "Chebucto" until Halifax was established by Governor [Edward Cornwallis](/wiki/Edward_Cornwallis "Edward Cornwallis") in 1749, named in honour of [George Montagu-Dunk, 2nd Earl of Halifax](/wiki/George_Montagu-Dunk,_2nd_Earl_of_Halifax "George Montagu-Dunk, 2nd Earl of Halifax").[[7]](#cite_note-names-7)[[8]](#cite_note-brown-8) The [Mi'kmaq](/wiki/Mi%27kmaq "Mi'kmaq") name for the harbour around which Halifax exists is *K'jipuktuk*[[9]](#cite_note-9) or *Chebookt*,[[a]](#cite_note-11) meaning "Chief Harbour" or "Great Harbour" in the [Mi'kmaq language](/wiki/Mi%27kmaq_language "Mi'kmaq language").[[7]](#cite_note-names-7)[[11]](#cite_note-randsilas-12) Additionally, the Mi'kmaq referred to the area of the Halifax Peninsula comprising the [downtown area](/wiki/Downtown_Halifax "Downtown Halifax") and [Point Pleasant Park](/wiki/Point_Pleasant_Park "Point Pleasant Park") as *Amntu'kati*,[[12]](#cite_note-13) while the hillside where Halifax was established was referred to as *Gooowakade*, meaning "place of many pines".[[10]](#cite_note-raddall-10)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Halifax,_Nova_Scotia&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

Main articles: [History of Halifax, Nova Scotia](/wiki/History_of_Halifax,_Nova_Scotia "History of Halifax, Nova Scotia"); [History of Dartmouth, Nova Scotia](/wiki/History_of_Dartmouth,_Nova_Scotia "History of Dartmouth, Nova Scotia"); [Bedford, Nova Scotia § History](/wiki/Bedford,_Nova_Scotia#History "Bedford, Nova Scotia"); and [Halifax County, Nova Scotia § History](/wiki/Halifax_County,_Nova_Scotia#History "Halifax County, Nova Scotia")

Before the mid-1700s, when a European (British) settlement was established at Halifax, the Colony of Nova Scotia had been administered from [Annapolis Royal](/wiki/Annapolis_Royal,_Nova_Scotia "Annapolis Royal, Nova Scotia"), near the Western opening of the [Bay of Fundy](/wiki/Bay_of_Fundy "Bay of Fundy"). However, in 1749, [King George II](/wiki/George_II_of_Great_Britain "George II of Great Britain") ordered [Edward Cornwallis](/wiki/Edward_Cornwallis "Edward Cornwallis") to establish Halifax as a new capital. Equipped with 13 transports and a sloop of war, Cornwallis founded the Town of Halifax on what is now known as the Halifax Peninsula, on June 21, 1749.[[13]](#cite_note-14)

The establishment of Halifax marked the beginning of [Father Le Loutre's War](/wiki/Father_Le_Loutre%27s_War "Father Le Loutre's War"). By unilaterally establishing Halifax, the British were violating treaties with the [Mi'kmaq](/wiki/Mi%27kmaq "Mi'kmaq") (1726) that were signed after [Father Rale's War](/wiki/Father_Rale%27s_War "Father Rale's War").[[14]](#cite_note-15) Cornwallis brought along 1,176 settlers and their families. To protect the new [Protestant](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism") settlements from Mi'kmaq, [Acadian](/wiki/Acadians "Acadians"), and French attacks, British fortifications were erected in Halifax ([Citadel Hill](/wiki/Citadel_Hill_(Fort_George) "Citadel Hill (Fort George)"), 1749), Bedford ([Fort Sackville](/wiki/Fort_Sackville_(Nova_Scotia) "Fort Sackville (Nova Scotia)"), 1749), Dartmouth (1750), and [Lawrencetown](/wiki/Lawrencetown,_Halifax_County,_Nova_Scotia "Lawrencetown, Halifax County, Nova Scotia") (1754), all areas within the modern-day Municipality.

The community of [St. Margaret's Bay](/wiki/St._Margarets_Bay,_Nova_Scotia "St. Margarets Bay, Nova Scotia"), also in the Municipality, was first settled by French-speaking [Foreign Protestants](/wiki/Foreign_Protestants "Foreign Protestants") at [French Village, Nova Scotia](/wiki/French_Village,_Nova_Scotia "French Village, Nova Scotia"), who migrated from [Lunenburg, Nova Scotia](/wiki/Lunenburg,_Nova_Scotia "Lunenburg, Nova Scotia"), during the American Revolution.

In the 18th and 19th centuries, Halifax grew to become a centre of government (particularly military), trade, and shipbuilding. The completion of the [Intercolonial Railway](/wiki/Intercolonial_Railway "Intercolonial Railway") in 1876 helped make Halifax a major gateway for Atlantic trade—particularly in winter, when ice made ports on the St. Lawrence River inaccessible.

This connection to trans-Atlantic trade and military enterprises bore deadly consequences on December 6, 1917. In one of the great disasters in Canadian history, the [SS *Mont-Blanc*](/wiki/SS_Mont-Blanc "SS Mont-Blanc"), a French cargo ship carrying munitions, collided with the [Belgian Relief](/wiki/Commission_for_Relief_in_Belgium "Commission for Relief in Belgium") vessel [SS *Imo*](/wiki/SS_Imo "SS Imo") in "The Narrows" between upper Halifax Harbour and Bedford Basin. The resulting [Halifax Explosion](/wiki/Halifax_Explosion "Halifax Explosion") devastated the [Richmond District](/wiki/Richmond,_Nova_Scotia "Richmond, Nova Scotia") in the North End of Halifax, killing about 2,000 people and injuring nearly 9,000 others.[[15]](#cite_note-cbc-16) The blast was the [largest artificial explosion](/wiki/List_of_the_largest_artificial_non-nuclear_explosions#Rank_order_of_largest_conventional_explosions#detonations_by_magnitude "List of the largest artificial non-nuclear explosions") before the development of nuclear weapons.[[16]](#cite_note-TimeDisaster-17) Aid came from [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston"), strengthening the [bond between the two coastal cities](/wiki/Boston%E2%80%93Halifax_relations "Boston–Halifax relations").

In addition to the capital City of Halifax (on the western side of the harbour), the City of Dartmouth (eastern side) and the Town of Bedford (northern tip of the Bedford Basin) grew up through the 19th and 20th centuries. Halifax County was originally understood as a higher-level geographic unit responsible for, among other things, court services throughout both the incorporated Cities and Town, and the unorganized territory of the remainder of the county. However, by the mid-20th century, this structure had evolved such that "the County" was a distinct municipality including only the areas that were *not* included within the Cities and Town.

These four municipalities (City of Halifax, City of Dartmouth, Town of Bedford, Halifax County) coordinated delivery of some services through a body known as the Metropolitan Authority starting in the late 1970s, but remained independent municipal bodies until April 1, 1996, when the provincial government [amalgamated](/wiki/Amalgamation_(politics) "Amalgamation (politics)") all municipal governments within [Halifax County](/wiki/Halifax_County,_Nova_Scotia "Halifax County, Nova Scotia") to create the Halifax Regional Municipality. Thus, the modern municipal boundary now includes all of Halifax County except for several Mi'kmaq reserves.[[17]](#cite_note-18)

Since amalgamation, the region has officially been known as the Halifax Regional Municipality (HRM), but "Halifax" has remained in common usage for brevity. In April 2014, the [Regional Council](/wiki/Halifax_Regional_Council "Halifax Regional Council") approved a new brand identity for the region, under which the name "Halifax" is used for marketing and communications purposes; "Halifax Regional Municipality" remains the region's official name.[[18]](#cite_note-ctv-newbrand-19)[[19]](#cite_note-ch-newlogo-20)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Halifax,_Nova_Scotia&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Geography")]

Main article: [Geography of Halifax, Nova Scotia](/wiki/Geography_of_Halifax,_Nova_Scotia "Geography of Halifax, Nova Scotia")

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Halifax,_Nova_Scotia&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Climate")]

Halifax has a [humid continental climate](/wiki/Humid_continental_climate "Humid continental climate") ([Köppen](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") *Dfb*), with warm summers and relatively mild winters, which is due to [Gulf Stream](/wiki/Gulf_Stream "Gulf Stream") moderation. The [weather](/wiki/Weather "Weather") is usually milder in the winter or cooler in the summer than areas at similar latitudes inland, with the temperature remaining (with occasional notable exceptions) between about −8 and 28 °C (18 and 82 °F).[[20]](#cite_note-21) January is the coldest month, being the only month with a high that is slightly below freezing at −0.1 °C (31.8 °F), while August is the warmest. The sea heavily influences the climate of the area, causing significant [seasonal lag](/wiki/Seasonal_lag "Seasonal lag") in summer, with August being significantly warmer than June and with September being the third mildest month in terms of mean temperature.[[21]](#cite_note-22)

[Hurricane Juan](/wiki/Hurricane_Juan "Hurricane Juan"), a [category 2](/wiki/Category_2_hurricane "Category 2 hurricane") storm, hit in September 2003 and caused considerable damage to the region. [Hurricane Earl](/wiki/Hurricane_Earl_(2010) "Hurricane Earl (2010)") grazed the coast as a [category 1](/wiki/Category_1_hurricane "Category 1 hurricane") storm in 2010. In 2019, [Hurricane Dorian](/wiki/Hurricane_Dorian "Hurricane Dorian") made landfall just south of Halifax as a post-tropical storm with an intensity equivalent to a category 2 hurricane and caused significant damage across Nova Scotia. In 2021 [Hurricane Ida](/wiki/Hurricane_Ida "Hurricane Ida") hit the region with minor damage. In 2022, [Hurricane Fiona](/wiki/Hurricane_Fiona "Hurricane Fiona") hit as a category 2 storm, although damage was relatively minor in Halifax, with downed trees and widespread power outages for days.[[22]](#cite_note-23)

The highest temperature ever recorded in Halifax was 37.2 °C (99.0 °F) on July 10, 1912,[[23]](#cite_note-24) and the lowest temperature recorded was −29.4 °C (−20.9 °F) on February 18, 1922.[[24]](#cite_note-25) In spite of the possibility of high temperatures, in a normal year there are only three days that go above 30 °C (86 °F).[[25]](#cite_note-Environment_Canada_1981-2010-26) Halifax also has a modest frost count by Canadian standards due to the maritime influence, averaging 131 air frosts and 49 full days below freezing annually.[[25]](#cite_note-Environment_Canada_1981-2010-26) On average the frost-free period is 182 days, ranging from May 1 to October 31.[[25]](#cite_note-Environment_Canada_1981-2010-26)

### Metropolitan landscape

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Halifax,_Nova_Scotia&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Metropolitan landscape")]

As of the [2021 Canadian Census](/wiki/2021_Canadian_Census "2021 Canadian Census"), the [Halifax Census Metropolitan Area](/wiki/Census_geographic_units_of_Canada "Census geographic units of Canada") includes all of [Halifax County](/wiki/Halifax_County,_Nova_Scotia "Halifax County, Nova Scotia"), together with the [Municipality of East Hants](/wiki/Municipality_of_the_District_of_East_Hants "Municipality of the District of East Hants"). The total land area of this metropolitan area is 727,622 hectares (7,276.22 square kilometres; 2,809.36 square miles)[[48]](#cite_note-auto-50)

In Canada, metro areas include neighbouring municipalities where more than half of the commuters commute into the core municipality. Between the [2016 Canadian Census](/wiki/2016_Canadian_Census "2016 Canadian Census") and the [2021 Canadian Census](/wiki/2021_Canadian_Census "2021 Canadian Census"), East Hants, a formerly rural, but increasingly exurban community located north of the main Halifax population centre, was added to the Halifax metro area, due to the increased population who commute from East Hants to Halifax. Before the [2021 Canadian Census](/wiki/2021_Canadian_Census "2021 Canadian Census"), Metropolitan Halifax covered 549,631 hectares (5,496.31 square kilometres; 2,122.14 square miles)[[49]](#cite_note-51) After the addition of the [East Hants](/wiki/Municipality_of_the_District_of_East_Hants "Municipality of the District of East Hants"), the metropolitan area's land area expanded by 177,991 hectares (1,779.91 km2) to its current land area.[[48]](#cite_note-auto-50)

### Municipal landscape

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Halifax,_Nova_Scotia&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Municipal landscape")]

Since 1 April 1996, the entirety of the [County of Halifax](/wiki/Halifax_County,_Nova_Scotia "Halifax County, Nova Scotia") and all its places (cities, suburbs, towns, and villages) were turned into communities of a larger single-tier municipality called Halifax Regional Municipality. As of 2021, the total surface area of the municipality is 5,475.57 km2 (2,114.13 sq mi).[[2]](#cite_note-statcan1-2)

#### Regional Centre

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Halifax,_Nova_Scotia&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Regional Centre")]

There are varying definitions of the "core" of Halifax. For planning purposes, the Municipality defines the "Regional Centre" as including the Halifax Peninsula and [Dartmouth](/wiki/Dartmouth,_Nova_Scotia "Dartmouth, Nova Scotia") inside the Circumferential Highway. This urban area covers 3,300 hectares (33 square kilometres; 13 square miles)[[50]](#cite_note-52) and houses 96,619 people in 55,332 dwelling units as of the 2016 Census.[[51]](#cite_note-53)

#### Communities and neighbourhoods

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Halifax,_Nova_Scotia&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Communities and neighbourhoods")]

Main article: [Communities in the Halifax Regional Municipality](/wiki/Communities_in_the_Halifax_Regional_Municipality "Communities in the Halifax Regional Municipality")

There are over 200 official communities and neighbourhoods within the Halifax municipality. The former [town](/wiki/Town "Town") of [Bedford](/wiki/Bedford,_Nova_Scotia "Bedford, Nova Scotia"), and the former cities of [Dartmouth](/wiki/Dartmouth,_Nova_Scotia "Dartmouth, Nova Scotia") and [Halifax](/wiki/History_of_Halifax_(former_city) "History of Halifax (former city)") have maintained their original geographic names. Furthermore, some communities that were [suburban](/wiki/Suburban "Suburban"), or even [rural](/wiki/Rural "Rural") before 1996, now have become more urban and have attained *community* status (e.g. [Cole Harbour](/wiki/Cole_Harbour,_Nova_Scotia "Cole Harbour, Nova Scotia"), [Lower Sackville](/wiki/Lower_Sackville,_Nova_Scotia "Lower Sackville, Nova Scotia"), [Spryfield](/wiki/Spryfield,_Nova_Scotia "Spryfield, Nova Scotia"), etc.)[[52]](#cite_note-54)

### Urban landscape

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Halifax,_Nova_Scotia&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Urban landscape")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/Halifax_at_night.jpg/250px-Halifax_at_night.jpg)](/wiki/File:Halifax_at_night.jpg)

View of [Purdy's Wharf](/wiki/Purdy%27s_Wharf "Purdy's Wharf"), an office complex in Downtown Halifax

At 23,829 hectares (238.29 square kilometres; 92.00 square miles), Halifax's [urban area](/wiki/Urban_area "Urban area") (defined as *population centre* by Statistics Canada) is less than five percent of the municipal land area.[[53]](#cite_note-auto1-55) The area surrounds [Halifax Harbour](/wiki/Halifax_Harbour "Halifax Harbour") and its main centres are [Bedford](/wiki/Bedford,_Nova_Scotia "Bedford, Nova Scotia"), [Dartmouth](/wiki/Dartmouth,_Nova_Scotia "Dartmouth, Nova Scotia"), and Halifax (and their respective environs).

Between the [2016 Canadian Census](/wiki/2016_Canadian_Census "2016 Canadian Census") and the [2021 Canadian Census](/wiki/2021_Canadian_Census "2021 Canadian Census"), the [built-up area](/wiki/Urban_area "Urban area") of Halifax grew by 357 hectares (3.57 km2) from 23,472 hectares (234.72 km2) in [2016](/wiki/2016_Canadian_Census "2016 Canadian Census")[[54]](#cite_note-56) to 23,829 hectares (238.29 km2) in [2021](/wiki/2021_Canadian_Census "2021 Canadian Census").[[53]](#cite_note-auto1-55)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9c/Art_Gallery_of_Nova_Scotia.JPG/250px-Art_Gallery_of_Nova_Scotia.JPG)](/wiki/File:Art_Gallery_of_Nova_Scotia.JPG)

The [Art Gallery of Nova Scotia](/wiki/Art_Gallery_of_Nova_Scotia "Art Gallery of Nova Scotia"), the largest [art gallery](/wiki/Art_gallery "Art gallery") in [Atlantic Canada](/wiki/Atlantic_Canada "Atlantic Canada")

Halifax is a major cultural centre within the [Atlantic provinces](/wiki/Atlantic_Canada "Atlantic Canada"). The municipality has maintained many of its maritime and military traditions, while opening itself to a growing multicultural population. The municipality's urban core also benefits from a large population of post-secondary students who strongly influence the local cultural scene. Halifax has a number of art galleries, theatres and museums, as well as most of the region's national-quality sports and entertainment facilities. Halifax is also the home to many of the region's major cultural attractions, such as [Halifax Pop Explosion](/wiki/Halifax_Pop_Explosion "Halifax Pop Explosion"), [Symphony Nova Scotia](/wiki/Symphony_Nova_Scotia "Symphony Nova Scotia"), the [Art Gallery of Nova Scotia](/wiki/Art_Gallery_of_Nova_Scotia "Art Gallery of Nova Scotia"), [The Khyber](/wiki/The_Khyber "The Khyber"), the [Maritime Museum of the Atlantic](/wiki/Maritime_Museum_of_the_Atlantic "Maritime Museum of the Atlantic") and the [Neptune Theatre (Halifax, Nova Scotia)](/wiki/Neptune_Theatre_(Halifax,_Nova_Scotia) "Neptune Theatre (Halifax, Nova Scotia)").[[55]](#cite_note-57)

The new [Halifax Central Library](/wiki/Halifax_Central_Library "Halifax Central Library") on [Spring Garden Road](/wiki/Spring_Garden,_Halifax "Spring Garden, Halifax") has received accolades for its architecture including the [Governor General's Medal in Architecture](/wiki/Governor_General%27s_Award "Governor General's Award") and was described by *Canadian Architect* as "the most significant public building completed in the Nova Scotia capital in over a generation, and a new cultural hub for the region".[[56]](#cite_note-canarch2016-58)

Main article: [Buildings and structures in Halifax, Nova Scotia](/wiki/Buildings_and_structures_in_Halifax,_Nova_Scotia "Buildings and structures in Halifax, Nova Scotia")

Further information: [List of tallest buildings in Halifax, Nova Scotia](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Halifax,_Nova_Scotia "List of tallest buildings in Halifax, Nova Scotia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/Halifax_Town_Clock_-_cdnav8r.jpg/250px-Halifax_Town_Clock_-_cdnav8r.jpg)](/wiki/File:Halifax_Town_Clock_-_cdnav8r.jpg)

The [Halifax Town Clock](/wiki/Halifax_Town_Clock "Halifax Town Clock") overlooks most of downtown Halifax.

Halifax's urban core is home to a number of regional landmark buildings and retains significant historic buildings and districts. Downtown office towers are overlooked by the fortress of [Citadel Hill](/wiki/Citadel_Hill_(Fort_George) "Citadel Hill (Fort George)") with its iconic [Halifax Town Clock](/wiki/Halifax_Town_Clock "Halifax Town Clock").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Public spaces

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Halifax,_Nova_Scotia&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Public spaces")]

Main article: [Parks in Halifax, Nova Scotia](/wiki/Parks_in_Halifax,_Nova_Scotia "Parks in Halifax, Nova Scotia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cc/CA-halifax-publ-garden-05.jpg/250px-CA-halifax-publ-garden-05.jpg)](/wiki/File:CA-halifax-publ-garden-05.jpg)

[Halifax Public Gardens](/wiki/Halifax_Public_Gardens "Halifax Public Gardens"), a [Victorian era](/wiki/Victorian_era "Victorian era") public garden that was designated as a [National Historic Site of Canada](/wiki/National_Historic_Sites_of_Canada "National Historic Sites of Canada") in 1984

The Halifax area has a variety of public spaces, ranging from urban gardens, public squares, expansive forested parks, and historic sites. The original grid plan devised when Halifax was founded in 1749 included a central [military parade](/wiki/Military_parade "Military parade") square, the [Grand Parade](/wiki/Grand_Parade_(Halifax) "Grand Parade (Halifax)"). The square hosts the City Hall at one end, and is a popular site for concerts, political demonstrations, as well as the annual [Remembrance Day](/wiki/Remembrance_Day "Remembrance Day") ceremony at the central [cenotaph](/wiki/Cenotaph "Cenotaph"). Another popular downtown public space is the timber [Halifax Boardwalk](/wiki/Halifax_Boardwalk "Halifax Boardwalk"), which stretches approximately 4-kilometre (2.5 mi) and is integrated with several squares and monuments.[[57]](#cite_note-59)

The [Halifax Common](/wiki/Halifax_Common "Halifax Common"), granted for the use of citizens in 1763, is Canada's oldest public park.[[58]](#cite_note-60) Centrally located on the Halifax Peninsula, the wide fields are a popular location for sports. The slopes of [Citadel Hill](/wiki/Citadel_Hill_(Fort_George) "Citadel Hill (Fort George)"), overlooking downtown, are favoured by sunbathers and kite-flyers. The [Halifax Public Gardens](/wiki/Halifax_Public_Gardens "Halifax Public Gardens"), a short walk away, are Victorian era public gardens formally established in 1867 and designated a [National Historic Site](/wiki/National_Historic_Sites_of_Canada "National Historic Sites of Canada") in 1984. [Victoria Park](/wiki/Victoria_Park,_Halifax,_Nova_Scotia "Victoria Park, Halifax, Nova Scotia"), across the street, contains various monuments and statues erected by the [North British Society](/wiki/North_British_Society "North British Society"), as well as a fountain. In contrast to the urban parks, the expansive [Point Pleasant Park](/wiki/Point_Pleasant_Park "Point Pleasant Park") at the southern tip of the peninsula is heavily forested and contains the remains of numerous British fortifications.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/American_Elm_Tree_in_Halifax_Public_Gardens_-_August_2019_01.jpg/250px-American_Elm_Tree_in_Halifax_Public_Gardens_-_August_2019_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:American_Elm_Tree_in_Halifax_Public_Gardens_-_August_2019_01.jpg)

An [elm](/wiki/Elm "Elm") tree in Halifax Public Gardens

Located on the opposite side of the harbour, the [Dartmouth Commons](/wiki/Dartmouth_Commons "Dartmouth Commons") is a large park next to [Downtown Dartmouth](/wiki/Downtown_Dartmouth "Downtown Dartmouth") laid out in the 1700s. It is home to the Leighton Dillman gardens and various sports grounds. Nearby, the Dartmouth waterfront trail stretches from Downtown Dartmouth to Woodside. Among residents of central Dartmouth, the area around [Sullivan's Pond](/wiki/Sullivan%27s_Pond "Sullivan's Pond") and [Lake Banook](/wiki/Lake_Banook "Lake Banook") is popular for strolling and paddling. The forested [Shubie Park](/wiki/Shubie_Park "Shubie Park"), through which the historic [Shubenacadie Canal](/wiki/Shubenacadie_Canal "Shubenacadie Canal") runs, is a major park in suburban Dartmouth.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Mainland Halifax is home to several significant parks, including [Sir Sandford Fleming Park](/wiki/Sir_Sandford_Fleming_Park "Sir Sandford Fleming Park"), gifted to the people of Halifax by Sir [Sandford Fleming](/wiki/Sandford_Fleming "Sandford Fleming"). It houses the Dingle Tower, dedicated in 1912 by the [Duke of Connaught](/wiki/Duke_of_Connaught_and_Strathearn "Duke of Connaught and Strathearn") to commemorate 150 years of representative government in Nova Scotia. The Mainland Common, in [Clayton Park](/wiki/Clayton_Park,_Nova_Scotia "Clayton Park, Nova Scotia"), is a modern park home to various sports and community facilities. [Long Lake Provincial Park](/wiki/Long_Lake_Provincial_Park_(Nova_Scotia) "Long Lake Provincial Park (Nova Scotia)"), comprising more than 2,000 hectares, was designated in 1984 and affords Halifax residents access to a scenic wilderness in close proximity to the urban communities.[[59]](#cite_note-61)

### Tourism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Halifax,_Nova_Scotia&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Tourism")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/Halifax%2C_NS%2C_Canada_-_panoramio_%286%29.jpg/250px-Halifax%2C_NS%2C_Canada_-_panoramio_%286%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Halifax,_NS,_Canada_-_panoramio_(6).jpg)

The community of [Peggys Cove](/wiki/Peggys_Cove,_Nova_Scotia "Peggys Cove, Nova Scotia"), a major tourist attraction

Halifax's tourism industry showcases Nova Scotia's culture, scenery and coastline. There are several museums and art galleries in downtown Halifax. The [Canadian Museum of Immigration at Pier 21](/wiki/Canadian_Museum_of_Immigration_at_Pier_21 "Canadian Museum of Immigration at Pier 21"), an immigrant entry point prominent throughout the 1930s, 1940s, and 1950s, was opened to the public as a [National Historic Site of Canada](/wiki/National_Historic_Site_of_Canada "National Historic Site of Canada") in 1999 and is the only [national museum](/wiki/National_museums_of_Canada "National museums of Canada") in the Atlantic provinces. The [Maritime Museum of the Atlantic](/wiki/Maritime_Museum_of_the_Atlantic "Maritime Museum of the Atlantic") is a [maritime museum](/wiki/Maritime_museum "Maritime museum") containing extensive galleries including a large exhibit on the famous [*Titanic*](/wiki/Titanic "Titanic"), over 70 small craft and a 200-foot (61 m) steamship [CSS *Acadia*](/wiki/CSS_Acadia "CSS Acadia"). In summertime the preserved World War II [corvette](/wiki/Flower-class_corvette "Flower-class corvette") [HMCS *Sackville*](/wiki/HMCS_Sackville "HMCS Sackville") operates as a museum ship and Canada's naval memorial. The [Art Gallery of Nova Scotia](/wiki/Art_Gallery_of_Nova_Scotia "Art Gallery of Nova Scotia") is housed in a 150-year-old building containing nearly 19,000 works of art.[[60]](#cite_note-nsart-62) The [Black Cultural Centre for Nova Scotia](/wiki/Black_Cultural_Centre_for_Nova_Scotia "Black Cultural Centre for Nova Scotia") in Dartmouth reflects the region's rich ethnic heritage.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/Halifax_boardwalk.JPG/250px-Halifax_boardwalk.JPG)](/wiki/File:Halifax_boardwalk.JPG)

The [Halifax Boardwalk](/wiki/Halifax_Boardwalk "Halifax Boardwalk"), a public [footpath](/wiki/Footpath "Footpath") along [Halifax Harbour](/wiki/Halifax_Harbour "Halifax Harbour")

Halifax has numerous [National Historic Sites](/wiki/National_Historic_Sites_of_Canada "National Historic Sites of Canada"), most notably [Citadel Hill (Fort George)](/wiki/Citadel_Hill_(Fort_George) "Citadel Hill (Fort George)"). Just outside the urban area, the iconic [Peggys Cove](/wiki/Peggys_Cove,_Nova_Scotia "Peggys Cove, Nova Scotia") is internationally recognized and receives more than 600,000 visitors a year.[[61]](#cite_note-63)

The waterfront in Downtown Halifax is the site of the [Halifax Boardwalk](/wiki/Halifax_Boardwalk "Halifax Boardwalk"), a 4-kilometre (2.5 mi) [boardwalk](/wiki/Boardwalk_(entertainment_district) "Boardwalk (entertainment district)")[[62]](#cite_note-64) popular among tourists and locals alike. Many mid-sized ships dock here at one of the many wharfs. The boardwalk is home to a [Halifax Transit](/wiki/Halifax_Transit "Halifax Transit") ferry terminal, hundreds of stores, [Historic Properties](/wiki/Historic_Properties_(Halifax) "Historic Properties (Halifax)"), several office buildings, the [Casino Nova Scotia](/wiki/Casino_Nova_Scotia "Casino Nova Scotia"), and several public squares where [buskers](/wiki/Busking "Busking") perform, most prominently at the annual Halifax Busker Festival every August over Nova Scotia’s [Natal Day](/wiki/Natal_Day "Natal Day") long weekend.[[63]](#cite_note-65)

Cruise ships also visit the municipality frequently. In 2024, the Port of Halifax welcomed 201 cruise ships with 360,000 passengers.[[64]](#cite_note-66)

### Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Halifax,_Nova_Scotia&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Media")]

Main article: [Media in Halifax, Nova Scotia](/wiki/Media_in_Halifax,_Nova_Scotia "Media in Halifax, Nova Scotia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/Halifax_Chronicle_Herald_building.jpg/250px-Halifax_Chronicle_Herald_building.jpg)](/wiki/File:Halifax_Chronicle_Herald_building.jpg)

Headquarters of *[The Chronicle Herald](/wiki/The_Chronicle_Herald "The Chronicle Herald")*, a daily newspaper published in Halifax

Halifax is the Atlantic region's central point for radio broadcast and press media. [CBC Television](/wiki/CBC_Television "CBC Television"), [CTV Television Network](/wiki/CTV_Television_Network "CTV Television Network") (CTV), and [Global Television Network](/wiki/Global_Television_Network "Global Television Network") and other broadcasters all have important regional television concentrators in the municipality. [CBC Radio](/wiki/CBC_Radio "CBC Radio") has a major regional studio and there are also regional hubs for Rogers Radio and various private broadcast franchises, as well as a regional bureau for [The Canadian Press](/wiki/The_Canadian_Press "The Canadian Press")/[Broadcast News](/wiki/The_Canadian_Press "The Canadian Press").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Halifax's print media is centred on its single daily newspaper, the broadsheet *[Chronicle Herald](/wiki/The_Chronicle_Herald "The Chronicle Herald")* as well as two free newspapers, the daily commuter-oriented edition of *[Metro International](/wiki/Metro_International "Metro International")* and the free alternative arts weekly *[The Coast](/wiki/The_Coast_(newspaper) "The Coast (newspaper)")*.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Halifax has several [online daily newspapers](/wiki/Online_newspaper "Online newspaper"). *[allNovaScotia](/wiki/AllNovaScotia "AllNovaScotia")* is a daily, subscriber-only outlet which focuses on business and political news from across the province.[[65]](#cite_note-brad-67) *CityNews Halifax*, associated with the [CJNI-FM](/wiki/CJNI-FM "CJNI-FM") radio station, was created from a merger of the former News 95.7 and *HalifaxToday* (previously *[Local Xpress](/wiki/Local_Xpress "Local Xpress")*) news websites. The *[Halifax Examiner](/wiki/Halifax_Examiner "Halifax Examiner")* was founded by the former news editor of *The Coast* in 2014 and, like *allNovaScotia*, is supported through subscriptions.

From 1974 to 2008, Halifax had a second daily newspaper, the tabloid *[The Daily News](/wiki/The_Daily_News_(Halifax) "The Daily News (Halifax)")*, which still publishes several neighbourhood weekly papers such as *The Bedford-Sackville Weekly News*, *The Halifax West-Clayton Park Weekly News* and the *Dartmouth-Cole Harbour Weekly News*. These weekly papers compete with *The Chronicle-Herald'*s weekly Community Heralds *HRM West*, *HRM East*, and *HRM North*.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Halifax,_Nova_Scotia&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Sports")]

Main article: [Sport in Halifax, Nova Scotia](/wiki/Sport_in_Halifax,_Nova_Scotia "Sport in Halifax, Nova Scotia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/Scotiabank_Centre_-_EXTERIOR_-_091914_-_Paul_Darrow_%283%29.JPG/250px-Scotiabank_Centre_-_EXTERIOR_-_091914_-_Paul_Darrow_%283%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:Scotiabank_Centre_-_EXTERIOR_-_091914_-_Paul_Darrow_(3).JPG)

The [Scotiabank Centre](/wiki/Scotiabank_Centre "Scotiabank Centre"), the largest [multi-purpose](/wiki/Multi-purpose_stadium "Multi-purpose stadium") sporting arena in Atlantic Canada

Halifax is represented by four professional sports teams, with teams in the [National Lacrosse League](/wiki/National_Lacrosse_League "National Lacrosse League") (NLL), the [Canadian Premier League](/wiki/Canadian_Premier_League "Canadian Premier League") (CPL), the [Northern Super League](/wiki/Northern_Super_League "Northern Super League") (NSL) and [The Basketball League](/wiki/The_Basketball_League "The Basketball League") (TBL). The [Halifax Mooseheads](/wiki/Halifax_Mooseheads "Halifax Mooseheads") play in the [Quebec Maritimes Junior Hockey League](/wiki/Quebec_Maritimes_Junior_Hockey_League "Quebec Maritimes Junior Hockey League") (QMJHL), which is part of the [Canadian Hockey League](/wiki/Canadian_Hockey_League "Canadian Hockey League") (CHL). It is also home to four universities that have athletic programmes.

Halifax's major sports venues include the [Scotiabank Centre](/wiki/Scotiabank_Centre "Scotiabank Centre") (formerly the Metro Centre[[66]](#cite_note-68)), the [Halifax Forum](/wiki/Halifax_Forum "Halifax Forum"), the [Wanderers Grounds](/wiki/Wanderers_Grounds "Wanderers Grounds") and various university sports facilities, such as [Huskies Stadium](/wiki/Huskies_Stadium "Huskies Stadium").

### Professional and semi-professional sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Halifax,_Nova_Scotia&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Professional and semi-professional sports")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/Halifax_Mooseheads.jpg/250px-Halifax_Mooseheads.jpg)](/wiki/File:Halifax_Mooseheads.jpg)

Halifax Mooseheads prepare for a 2012 semi-final game.

Halifax is home to the [Halifax Mooseheads](/wiki/Halifax_Mooseheads "Halifax Mooseheads"), the semi-professional [major junior](/wiki/Junior_ice_hockey#Major_junior "Junior ice hockey") [hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey") club of the [Quebec Maritimes Junior Hockey League](/wiki/Quebec_Maritimes_Junior_Hockey_League "Quebec Maritimes Junior Hockey League") (QMJHL). Founded in 1994, the Mooseheads were the first team from [Atlantic Canada](/wiki/Atlantic_Canada "Atlantic Canada") to join the QMJHL. They began play in the league's Dilio Division in the [1994–95 season](/wiki/1994%E2%80%9395_QMJHL_season "1994–95 QMJHL season"),[[67]](#cite_note-history-69) In 2013, the Mooseheads capped a 74-win season (74–7–3–1[[68]](#cite_note-70)) with a QMJHL's [President's Cup](/wiki/President%27s_Cup_(QMJHL) "President's Cup (QMJHL)") championship. While hosting the tournament, the Mooseheads also won the CHL's [2013](/wiki/2013_Memorial_Cup "2013 Memorial Cup") [Memorial Cup](/wiki/Memorial_Cup "Memorial Cup").[[69]](#cite_note-71) They have appeared in the President's Cup Finals four more times: [2003](/wiki/2002%E2%80%9303_QMJHL_season#Playoffs "2002–03 QMJHL season"), [2005](/wiki/2004%E2%80%9305_QMJHL_season#Playoffs "2004–05 QMJHL season"), [2019](/wiki/2018%E2%80%9319_QMJHL_season#Playoffs "2018–19 QMJHL season") and [2023](/wiki/2022%E2%80%9323_QMJHL_season#Playoffs "2022–23 QMJHL season"). They hosted the [Memorial Cup](/wiki/Memorial_Cup "Memorial Cup") tournaments two more times in [2000](/wiki/2000_Memorial_Cup "2000 Memorial Cup") and [2019](/wiki/2019_Memorial_Cup "2019 Memorial Cup").

The [Halifax Thunderbirds](/wiki/Halifax_Thunderbirds "Halifax Thunderbirds") became the city's [National Lacrosse League](/wiki/National_Lacrosse_League "National Lacrosse League") team in September 2018, when it moved from [Rochester](/wiki/Rochester,_New_York "Rochester, New York").[[70]](#cite_note-72) On March 12, 2020, their inaugural season was cut short by the COVID-19 pandemic.[[71]](#cite_note-73) Still, the team received two league awards: Defensive Player of the Year and Executive of the Year.[[72]](#cite_note-74)[[73]](#cite_note-75) In the 2021–2022 season, they reached their first playoffs, where they were defeated by the [Toronto Rock](/wiki/Toronto_Rock "Toronto Rock") 14–13 in overtime[[74]](#cite_note-76) in the conference semi-final.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ef/HFX_Wanderers_FC_vs._Cavalry_FC_%2848174115731%29.jpg/250px-HFX_Wanderers_FC_vs._Cavalry_FC_%2848174115731%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:HFX_Wanderers_FC_vs._Cavalry_FC_(48174115731).jpg)

Canadian Premier League action in June 2019

In 2019, [HFX Wanderers FC](/wiki/HFX_Wanderers_FC "HFX Wanderers FC") debuted as an expansion club in the [Canadian Premier League](/wiki/Canadian_Premier_League "Canadian Premier League") (CPL)—Canada's professional men's soccer league.[[75]](#cite_note-first_ever_CPL_victory-77) In 2025, [Halifax Tides FC](/wiki/Halifax_Tides_FC "Halifax Tides FC") debuted as a founding member of the [Northern Super League](/wiki/Northern_Super_League "Northern Super League") (NSL), a newly established professional women's soccer league.[[76]](#cite_note-78)

The [Halifax Hoopers](/wiki/Halifax_Hoopers "Halifax Hoopers") began play in [The Basketball League](/wiki/The_Basketball_League "The Basketball League") in 2025, returning professional basketball to the area after the closure of the [Halifax Hurricanes](/wiki/Halifax_Hurricanes "Halifax Hurricanes").[[77]](#cite_note-79)

Halifax has been a recurring subject of proposed expansion teams in the [Canadian Football League](/wiki/Canadian_Football_League "Canadian Football League"); the [Atlantic Schooners](/wiki/Atlantic_Schooners "Atlantic Schooners") were proposed in the 1980s as a Halifax-based expansion team that would play out of Dartmouth, but folded after failing to secure funding for a stadium.[[78]](#cite_note-80) A second attempt at the Schooners was pursued by an ownership group known as [Schooners Sports and Entertainment](/wiki/Schooners_Sports_and_Entertainment "Schooners Sports and Entertainment") in the 2010s; in an effort to gauge interest in the franchise, the group hosted the neutral-site [Touchdown Atlantic](/wiki/Touchdown_Atlantic "Touchdown Atlantic") game at [Huskies Stadium](/wiki/Huskies_Stadium "Huskies Stadium") in 2023, marking the first regular-season CFL game played in Halifax.[[79]](#cite_note-81) The Schooners Sports and Entertainment bid also collapsed, after the ownership group was unable to secure a deal for a stadium in Dartmouth.[[80]](#cite_note-82)[[81]](#cite_note-CP_2005-06-11-83)

### Former professional teams

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Halifax,_Nova_Scotia&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Former professional teams")]

The city had a team in the [National Basketball League of Canada](/wiki/National_Basketball_League_of_Canada "National Basketball League of Canada") (NBL Canada) called the [Halifax Hurricanes](/wiki/Halifax_Hurricanes "Halifax Hurricanes"). The team succeeded the [Halifax Rainmen](/wiki/Halifax_Rainmen "Halifax Rainmen") who had previously played in the [American Basketball Association](/wiki/American_Basketball_Association_(2000%E2%80%93present) "American Basketball Association (2000–present)") and [Premier Basketball League](/wiki/Premier_Basketball_League "Premier Basketball League") before joining the NBL Canada and later declaring bankruptcy in July 2015.[[82]](#cite_note-84) The Hurricanes won the NBL Canada championship in their inaugural season. The Hurricanes have since ceased operations.[[83]](#cite_note-85)

Between 1971 and 1993, Halifax was home to three teams in the [American Hockey League](/wiki/American_Hockey_League "American Hockey League"). The [Nova Scotia Voyageurs](/wiki/Nova_Scotia_Voyageurs "Nova Scotia Voyageurs") ([Montreal Canadiens](/wiki/Montreal_Canadiens "Montreal Canadiens") affiliate), the [Nova Scotia Oilers](/wiki/Nova_Scotia_Oilers "Nova Scotia Oilers") ([Edmonton Oilers](/wiki/Edmonton_Oilers "Edmonton Oilers") affiliate) and finally, the [Halifax Citadels](/wiki/Halifax_Citadels "Halifax Citadels") ([Quebec Nordiques](/wiki/Quebec_Nordiques "Quebec Nordiques") affiliate) played from 1971 to 1984, 1984 to 1988, and 1988 to 1993, respectively.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The [Nova Scotia Clippers](/wiki/Nova_Scotia_Clippers "Nova Scotia Clippers") were a team in the original [Canadian Soccer League (1987–1992)](/wiki/Canadian_Soccer_League_(1987%E2%80%931992) "Canadian Soccer League (1987–1992)") for its 1991 season.

The [Halifax Crescents](/wiki/Halifax_Crescents "Halifax Crescents"), an amateur and later, professional ice hockey team challenged for the [Stanley Cup](/wiki/Stanley_Cup "Stanley Cup") in [1900](/wiki/1900_CAHL_season "1900 CAHL season") but lost to the [Montreal Shamrocks](/wiki/Montreal_Shamrocks "Montreal Shamrocks").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### University sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Halifax,_Nova_Scotia&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: University sports")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/Huskies_Stadium_in_Halifax%2C_Nova_Scotia%2C_Canada.jpg/250px-Huskies_Stadium_in_Halifax%2C_Nova_Scotia%2C_Canada.jpg)](/wiki/File:Huskies_Stadium_in_Halifax,_Nova_Scotia,_Canada.jpg)

Huskies Stadium in 2018

Halifax is home to seven degree-granting [post-secondary](/wiki/Post-secondary "Post-secondary") educational institutions with four of them having athletic programmes. Two of the schools, [Dalhousie University](/wiki/Dalhousie_University "Dalhousie University") and [Saint Mary's University](/wiki/Saint_Mary%27s_University_(Halifax) "Saint Mary's University (Halifax)") are part of the [U Sports](/wiki/U_Sports "U Sports") league, while [Mount Saint Vincent University](/wiki/Mount_Saint_Vincent_University "Mount Saint Vincent University") and [University of King's College](/wiki/University_of_King%27s_College "University of King's College") are a part of the [Canadian Collegiate Athletic Association](/wiki/Canadian_Collegiate_Athletic_Association "Canadian Collegiate Athletic Association") (CCAA).

Dalhousie University's varsity team goes by the moniker [Tigers](/wiki/Dalhousie_Tigers "Dalhousie Tigers"). They have teams for basketball, hockey, soccer, swimming, track and field, cross country running, and volleyball. The Tigers garnered a number of championships in the first decade of the 20th century, winning 63 AUS championships and two U Sports championships.[[84]](#cite_note-86)

Halifax's other U Sports university, located in close proximity to Dalhousie University, is Saint Mary's University with the moniker of the [Huskies](/wiki/Saint_Mary%27s_Huskies "Saint Mary's Huskies"). Known for their football programme, the Huskies play at [Huskies Stadium](/wiki/Huskies_Stadium "Huskies Stadium") and won back-to-back [Canadian University Football Championships](/wiki/Vanier_Cup "Vanier Cup") (2001 & 2002), only the third university to do so.[[85]](#cite_note-87)

Finally, two of Halifax's smaller universities are part of the [Canadian Collegiate Athletic Association](/wiki/Canadian_Collegiate_Athletic_Association "Canadian Collegiate Athletic Association") (CCAA). [Mount Saint Vincent University](/wiki/Mount_Saint_Vincent_University "Mount Saint Vincent University"), home to the Mystics, competes in the [Atlantic Collegiate Athletic Association](/wiki/Atlantic_Collegiate_Athletic_Association "Atlantic Collegiate Athletic Association") (ACAA), a member of the CCAA, in Women's & Men's Basketball, Women's & Men's Soccer, Cross Country and Women's Volleyball.[[86]](#cite_note-88) The Mystics hold a championship titles in all sports, making them the most acclaimed team of the ACAA division. [University of King's College](/wiki/University_of_King%27s_College "University of King's College") is also a member of the ACAA. The varsity athletics teams at the University of King's College are named the Blue Devils.[[87]](#cite_note-89) Sporting teams include men's and women's basketball, soccer, badminton and rugby, and women's volleyball.[[88]](#cite_note-90)

From 1984 to 2007, the region was home to the [CIS Men's Basketball Championship](/wiki/Canadian_Interuniversity_Sport_men%27s_basketball_champions "Canadian Interuniversity Sport men's basketball champions"); the tournament was moved to [Ottawa](/wiki/Ottawa "Ottawa"), [Ontario](/wiki/Ontario "Ontario"), from 2008 to 2010 and returned to Halifax in 2011 and 2012.

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Halifax,_Nova_Scotia&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Economy")]

Main article: [Economy of Halifax, Nova Scotia](/wiki/Economy_of_Halifax,_Nova_Scotia "Economy of Halifax, Nova Scotia")

The urban area of Halifax is a major economic centre in eastern Canada with a large concentration of government services and private sector companies. Halifax serves as the business, banking, government and cultural centre for the Maritime region. The largest employment sectors within the municipality include trade (36,400 jobs), health care and social
assistance (31,800 jobs), professional services (19,000 jobs), education (17,400 jobs), and public administration (15,800 jobs).[[103]](#cite_note-introhalifax-110) The Halifax economy is growing, with the [Conference Board of Canada](/wiki/Conference_Board_of_Canada "Conference Board of Canada") predicting strong 3.0% GDP growth for 2015.[[103]](#cite_note-introhalifax-110)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/db/Halifax_Shipyard_July_2017.jpg/250px-Halifax_Shipyard_July_2017.jpg)](/wiki/File:Halifax_Shipyard_July_2017.jpg)

The [Halifax Shipyard](/wiki/Halifax_Shipyard "Halifax Shipyard") of [Irving Shipbuilding](/wiki/Irving_Shipbuilding "Irving Shipbuilding"), a major employer in Halifax

Major employers and economic generators include the [Department of National Defence](/wiki/Canadian_Forces "Canadian Forces"), the [Port of Halifax](/wiki/Halifax_Port_Authority "Halifax Port Authority"), [Irving Shipbuilding](/wiki/Irving_Shipbuilding "Irving Shipbuilding"), the [Nova Scotia Health Authority](/wiki/Nova_Scotia_Health_Authority "Nova Scotia Health Authority"), IMP Group, [Bell Aliant](/wiki/Bell_Aliant "Bell Aliant"), [Emera](/wiki/Emera "Emera"), the [Bedford Institute of Oceanography](/wiki/Bedford_Institute_of_Oceanography "Bedford Institute of Oceanography"), government, banks, and universities.[[104]](#cite_note-111) The municipality has a growing concentration of manufacturing industries and is becoming a major multi-modal transportation hub through growth at the port, the [Halifax Stanfield International Airport](/wiki/Halifax_Stanfield_International_Airport "Halifax Stanfield International Airport"), and improving rail and highway connections. Halifax is one of Canada's top four container ports in terms of the volume of cargo handled.[[105]](#cite_note-112) A real estate boom in recent years has led to numerous new property developments, including the [gentrification](/wiki/Gentrification "Gentrification") of some former working-class areas.[[103]](#cite_note-introhalifax-110)

Agriculture, fishing, mining, forestry and natural gas extraction are major resource industries found in the rural areas of the municipality. Halifax's largest agricultural district is in the [Musquodoboit Valley](/wiki/Musquodoboit_Valley "Musquodoboit Valley"); the total number of farms in Halifax is 150, of which 110 are family-owned. Fishing harbours are located along all coastal areas with some having an independent harbour authority, such as the [Sheet Harbour](/wiki/Sheet_Harbour "Sheet Harbour") Industrial Port,[[106]](#cite_note-113) and others being managed as small craft harbours under the federal [Fisheries and Oceans Canada](/wiki/Fisheries_and_Oceans_Canada "Fisheries and Oceans Canada").

## Government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Halifax,_Nova_Scotia&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Government")]

Main article: [Government in the Halifax Regional Municipality](/wiki/Government_in_the_Halifax_Regional_Municipality "Government in the Halifax Regional Municipality")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/69/Halifax_-_NS_-_Rathaus_Halifax.jpg/250px-Halifax_-_NS_-_Rathaus_Halifax.jpg)](/wiki/File:Halifax_-_NS_-_Rathaus_Halifax.jpg)

[Halifax City Hall](/wiki/Halifax_City_Hall "Halifax City Hall"), the [seat of municipal government](/wiki/Halifax_Regional_Council "Halifax Regional Council")

The Halifax Regional Municipality is governed by a [mayor](/wiki/Mayor_of_the_Halifax_Regional_Municipality "Mayor of the Halifax Regional Municipality") (elected at large) and a sixteen-person [council](/wiki/Halifax_Regional_Council "Halifax Regional Council"). Councillors are elected by geographic district, with municipal elections occurring every four years. The current mayor of Halifax is [Andy Fillmore](/wiki/Andy_Fillmore "Andy Fillmore"). The [Halifax Regional Council](/wiki/Halifax_Regional_Council "Halifax Regional Council") is responsible for all facets of [municipal government](/wiki/Municipal_government "Municipal government"), including the [Halifax Regional Police](/wiki/Halifax_Regional_Police "Halifax Regional Police"), [Halifax Public Libraries](/wiki/Halifax_Public_Libraries "Halifax Public Libraries"), [Halifax Fire and Emergency](/wiki/Halifax_Fire_and_Emergency "Halifax Fire and Emergency"), [Halifax Regional Water Commission](/wiki/Halifax_Regional_Water_Commission "Halifax Regional Water Commission"), parks and recreation, civic addressing, public works, waste management, and planning and development.[[107]](#cite_note-HRMCharter-114) The provincial legislation that provides governance oversight to the municipality is the Halifax Regional Municipality Charter.[[107]](#cite_note-HRMCharter-114) The municipality has a proposed operating budget of $869 million for 2015–2016.[[108]](#cite_note-115)

The municipality also has four [community councils](/wiki/Halifax_Regional_Community_Council "Halifax Regional Community Council") that consider local matters. Each community council comprises five or six regional councillors representing neighbouring districts.[[109]](#cite_note-116) Most community council decisions are subject to final approval by regional council.[[107]](#cite_note-HRMCharter-114)

As the capital of Nova Scotia, Halifax is also the meeting place of the [Nova Scotia House of Assembly](/wiki/Nova_Scotia_House_of_Assembly "Nova Scotia House of Assembly"), the oldest assembly in Canada and the site of the first [responsible government](/wiki/Responsible_government "Responsible government") in British North America.[[110]](#cite_note-117) The legislature meets in [Province House](/wiki/Province_House_(Nova_Scotia) "Province House (Nova Scotia)"), a nearly 200-year-old [National Historic Site](/wiki/National_Historic_Sites_of_Canada "National Historic Sites of Canada") in downtown Halifax hailed as one of the finest examples of [Palladian architecture](/wiki/Palladian_architecture "Palladian architecture") in North America.[[111]](#cite_note-118)

The municipality is represented in the House of Commons by six MPs, elected in the ridings of [Halifax](/wiki/Halifax_(federal_electoral_district) "Halifax (federal electoral district)"), [Halifax West](/wiki/Halifax_West "Halifax West"), [Dartmouth—Cole Harbour](/wiki/Dartmouth%E2%80%94Cole_Harbour "Dartmouth—Cole Harbour"), [Sackville—Bedford—Preston](/wiki/Sackville%E2%80%94Bedford%E2%80%94Preston "Sackville—Bedford—Preston"), [South Shore—St. Margarets](/wiki/South_Shore%E2%80%94St._Margarets "South Shore—St. Margarets"), and [Central Nova](/wiki/Central_Nova "Central Nova"). The latter two ridings also include rural areas outside the municipality.[[112]](#cite_note-119)

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Halifax,_Nova_Scotia&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Education")]

Main article: [Education in Halifax, Nova Scotia](/wiki/Education_in_Halifax,_Nova_Scotia "Education in Halifax, Nova Scotia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/Henri_Hicks_Building%2C_Dalhousie_University.jpg/250px-Henri_Hicks_Building%2C_Dalhousie_University.jpg)](/wiki/File:Henri_Hicks_Building,_Dalhousie_University.jpg)

Established in 1818, [Dalhousie University](/wiki/Dalhousie_University "Dalhousie University") is among the oldest English-language post-secondary institutions in Canada.

Halifax has a well-developed network of public and private schools, providing instruction from grade pre-primary to grade twelve; 137 public schools are administered by the [Halifax Regional Centre for Education](/wiki/Halifax_Regional_Centre_for_Education "Halifax Regional Centre for Education"), while eight public schools are administered by the [Conseil scolaire acadien provincial](/wiki/Conseil_scolaire_acadien_provincial "Conseil scolaire acadien provincial").[[113]](#cite_note-120) The municipality's fourteen private schools are operated independently.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Halifax,_Nova_Scotia&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Transportation")]

Main article: [Transportation in Halifax, Nova Scotia](/wiki/Transportation_in_Halifax,_Nova_Scotia "Transportation in Halifax, Nova Scotia")

### Air

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Halifax,_Nova_Scotia&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Air")]

[Halifax Stanfield International Airport](/wiki/Halifax_Stanfield_International_Airport "Halifax Stanfield International Airport") serves Halifax and most of the province, providing scheduled flights to domestic and international destinations. The airport served 3,107,425 passengers in 2022, making it Canada's [sixth busiest airport](/wiki/List_of_the_busiest_airports_in_Canada "List of the busiest airports in Canada") by passenger traffic.[[114]](#cite_note-121) [Shearwater](/wiki/CFB_Shearwater "CFB Shearwater"), part of [CFB Halifax](/wiki/CFB_Halifax "CFB Halifax"), is the air base for maritime helicopters employed by the [Royal Canadian Navy](/wiki/Royal_Canadian_Navy "Royal Canadian Navy") and is located on the eastern side of Halifax Harbour.

The bike lane itself is at the same level as the sidewalk, on the right.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d6/Protected_bike_lane_on_Lower_Water_Street_in_Halifax_2021_4.jpg/250px-Protected_bike_lane_on_Lower_Water_Street_in_Halifax_2021_4.jpg)](/wiki/File:Protected_bike_lane_on_Lower_Water_Street_in_Halifax_2021_4.jpg)

Dedicated bike lane in downtown Halifax

Main article: [Cycling in Halifax, Nova Scotia](/wiki/Cycling_in_Halifax,_Nova_Scotia "Cycling in Halifax, Nova Scotia")

In recent years, the municipality has also begun to place increased emphasis on developing bicycling infrastructure. Halifax has developed 100 km (62 mi) of bikeways, 89 km (55 mi) of which are dedicated bicycle lanes.[[115]](#cite_note-122)

### Road

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Halifax,_Nova_Scotia&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Road")]

The urban core is linked by the [Angus L. Macdonald](/wiki/Angus_L._Macdonald_Bridge "Angus L. Macdonald Bridge") and [A. Murray MacKay](/wiki/A._Murray_MacKay_Bridge "A. Murray MacKay Bridge") suspension bridges, as well as the network of [100-series highways](/wiki/100-series_highways_(Nova_Scotia) "100-series highways (Nova Scotia)") which function as expressways. Furthermore, [Nova Scotia Trunk 7](/wiki/Nova_Scotia_Trunk_7 "Nova Scotia Trunk 7") and [Nova Scotia Trunk 2](/wiki/Nova_Scotia_Trunk_2 "Nova Scotia Trunk 2") serve as major road in the area. [Nova Scotia Highway 111](/wiki/Nova_Scotia_Highway_111 "Nova Scotia Highway 111") serves as a partial northwestern [beltway](/wiki/Ring_road "Ring road") of the city.[[116]](#cite_note-123)

### Public transit

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Halifax,_Nova_Scotia&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Public transit")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/Halifax_Transit_ferry_heading_towards_downtown.jpg/250px-Halifax_Transit_ferry_heading_towards_downtown.jpg)](/wiki/File:Halifax_Transit_ferry_heading_towards_downtown.jpg)

[Halifax Transit](/wiki/Halifax_Transit "Halifax Transit") ferry, Craig Blake, departing from [Dartmouth](/wiki/Dartmouth,_Nova_Scotia "Dartmouth, Nova Scotia"), [Nova Scotia](/wiki/Nova_Scotia "Nova Scotia"), [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada").

Public transit is provided by [Halifax Transit](/wiki/Halifax_Transit "Halifax Transit"), which operates standard bus routes, regional express bus routes, as well as the pedestrian-only [Halifax-Dartmouth Ferry Service](/wiki/Halifax-Dartmouth_Ferry_Service "Halifax-Dartmouth Ferry Service"). Established in 1752, the municipality's ferry service is the oldest continuously running [salt water](/wiki/Seawater "Seawater") ferry service in North America.[[117]](#cite_note-124)

### Rail

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Halifax,_Nova_Scotia&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Rail")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8f/Pier21now.JPG/250px-Pier21now.JPG)](/wiki/File:Pier21now.JPG)

The [Port of Halifax](/wiki/Port_of_Halifax "Port of Halifax") is North America's first inbound and last outbound shipping gateway to Europe.

The Halifax Port Authority's various shipping terminals constitute the eastern terminus of [Canadian National Railway](/wiki/Canadian_National_Railway "Canadian National Railway")'s transcontinental network. [Via Rail Canada](/wiki/Via_Rail "Via Rail") provides overnight passenger rail service from the [Halifax Railway Station](/wiki/Halifax,_Nova_Scotia_railway_station "Halifax, Nova Scotia railway station") three days a week to [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal") with the *[Ocean](/wiki/Ocean_(train) "Ocean (train)")*, a train equipped with sleeper cars that stops in major centres along the way, such as Moncton. The Halifax Railway Station also serves as the terminus for [Maritime Bus](/wiki/Maritime_Bus "Maritime Bus"), which serves destinations across the Maritimes.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Water

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Halifax,_Nova_Scotia&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Water")]

[Halifax Harbour](/wiki/Halifax_Harbour "Halifax Harbour") is a major port used by numerous shipping lines, administered by the [Halifax Port Authority](/wiki/Halifax_Port_Authority "Halifax Port Authority"). The [Royal Canadian Navy](/wiki/Royal_Canadian_Navy "Royal Canadian Navy") and the [Canadian Coast Guard](/wiki/Canadian_Coast_Guard "Canadian Coast Guard") have major installations along prominent sections of coastline in both Halifax and Dartmouth. The harbour is also home to a public ferry service connecting downtown Halifax to two locations in Dartmouth, with plans in development to add an additional ferry route connecting downtown and Bedford by 2028.[[118]](#cite_note-125)

Sheet Harbour is the other major port in the municipality and serves industrial users on the [Eastern Shore](/wiki/Eastern_Shore_(Nova_Scotia) "Eastern Shore (Nova Scotia)").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Sister cities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Halifax,_Nova_Scotia&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Sister cities")]

* [Hakodate](/wiki/Hakodate "Hakodate"), Japan (1982). The cities chose to twin because they both have [star forts](/wiki/Star_fort "Star fort") and are both maritime ports. Halifax has donated many fir trees to the annual Hakodate Christmas Fantasy festival.[[119]](#cite_note-126)[[120]](#cite_note-127)
* [Campeche](/wiki/Campeche,_Campeche "Campeche, Campeche"), Mexico (1999). Campeche was chosen because, like Halifax, it is "a capital of a state" and is "a city of similar size to Halifax on or near the coast having rich historical tradition".[[121]](#cite_note-128)
* [Norfolk](/wiki/Norfolk,_Virginia "Norfolk, Virginia"), [Virginia](/wiki/Virginia "Virginia"), United States (2006). Norfolk was chosen because, like Halifax, its economy "depends heavily on the presence of the Armed Forces, and both cities are very proud of their military history".[[122]](#cite_note-129)
* [Portsmouth](/wiki/Portsmouth "Portsmouth"), England (2023). Halifax and Portsmouth signed a sister city agreement in early 2023. They chose to twin as both cities are historic naval and shipbuilding centres. Additionally, both cities are centres for university education.[[123]](#cite_note-130)[[124]](#cite_note-131)
